title: Your body's defences — how you fight infection
examTip: AQA tests this in two ways: 'describe the non-specific defences' (barriers) and 'compare phagocytes and lymphocytes'. Know both types of white blood cell — what they do, how they do it, and how they differ. Immunological memory links directly to the vaccination topic.
introTips: This lesson has two layers: the physical barriers that stop pathogens getting in, and the white blood cells that deal with anything that does get in.
Phagocytes and lymphocytes are both white blood cells but they work in completely different ways — that comparison is a favourite exam question.
The memory cells idea at the end is the biological reason vaccination works — keep that connection in mind.
intro: Every second of every day, pathogens are trying to get into your body. Most of them never make it — not because you're lucky, but because you have several layers of defence working constantly. Understanding those layers tells you exactly why some infections take hold and others don't.

keyPoints.heading: Physical barriers — your first line of defence
keyPoints.content: Before pathogens can cause disease, they have to get inside your body. Your first line of defence makes that as difficult as possible. Skin is the body's largest barrier — a continuous, tough outer layer that most pathogens simply cannot penetrate as long as it's intact. If you cut your skin, that barrier is broken, which is why wounds get infected. In your respiratory tract (nose, throat, airways), mucus is produced which traps pathogens before they can reach your lungs. Tiny hair-like structures called cilia line the airways and beat rhythmically to sweep mucus — and the trapped pathogens — up and out of the airway, where they can be swallowed. In the stomach, hydrochloric acid (HCl) creates an extremely acidic environment of around pH 2. Most pathogens that enter through your mouth are killed here before they can reach the intestines.
writeBullets: Skin: continuous physical barrier; prevents pathogen entry if intact
Mucus: produced in airways; traps pathogens
Cilia: tiny hairs in airways; beat to sweep mucus (and trapped pathogens) upwards and out
Stomach acid (HCl, ~pH 2): kills most ingested pathogens before they reach the gut

keyTerms.def: A sticky secretion that lines the respiratory tract and traps pathogens before they can reach the lungs
Tiny hair-like projections on cells lining the airways that beat rhythmically to sweep mucus and trapped pathogens upwards
The acid produced in the stomach (approx. pH 2) that kills most pathogens in swallowed food and drink
A defence mechanism that acts against all pathogens, regardless of type, without needing prior exposure

keyPoints.memTip: Three-part list: Skin stops them entering, Mucus traps them, Cilia sweep them away, and Stomach acid kills anything you swallow. Say it as SMS: Skin – Mucus/cilia – Stomach acid. In an exam, name each one AND say what it does — one mark per defence, one mark per function.

keyPoints.heading: White blood cells — your immune army
keyPoints.content: If a pathogen gets past the physical barriers, your immune system takes over. The key players are white blood cells (also called leucocytes). Unlike red blood cells, white blood cells have a nucleus and are part of the body's defence system. There are two main types that AQA focuses on: phagocytes and lymphocytes. They both patrol your blood and tissues, but they have very different strategies for dealing with pathogens. It is important to understand that white blood cells do not contain haemoglobin and do not carry oxygen — that is the job of red blood cells.
writeBullets: White blood cells (leucocytes) are part of the immune system
Unlike red blood cells, white blood cells have a nucleus
Two main types: phagocytes and lymphocytes — different jobs
Both patrol blood and tissues, attacking foreign material

keyTerms.def: A blood cell that forms part of the immune system; includes phagocytes and lymphocytes
The scientific name for a white blood cell
The body's system of cells and mechanisms that identifies and destroys pathogens and foreign material

keyPoints.memTip: White blood cells = immune system. Red blood cells = oxygen transport. Never mix these up. If an exam question mentions antibodies, antitoxins, or engulfing — that's white blood cells. If it mentions haemoglobin — that's red blood cells.

keyPoints.heading: Phagocytes — engulf and destroy
keyPoints.content: Phagocytes are the fast-response unit of your immune system. When they detect something foreign — a bacterium, a dead cell, debris — they engulf it by changing shape to surround it, pulling it inside the cell. Once inside, the pathogen is digested by enzymes. This process is called phagocytosis. Phagocytes are non-specific: they don't need to recognise a particular pathogen — they attack anything that isn't a normal body cell. This makes them the body's first responder when an infection starts.
writeBullets: Phagocytes engulf (surround) pathogens and pull them inside the cell
Enzymes digest the pathogen once engulfed
Process = phagocytosis
Non-specific: attack any foreign material, no prior exposure needed
Quick first responders

keyTerms.def: A white blood cell that engulfs and digests pathogens and other foreign material by phagocytosis
The process by which a phagocyte surrounds and engulfs a pathogen, then digests it with enzymes
Acting against all pathogens equally, without requiring a specific match or prior exposure

keyPoints.memTip: Phagocyte = PHAG (from Greek phagein, 'to eat') + CYTE (cell) = eating cell. Phagocytosis = the eating process. To remember it's non-specific: phagocytes don't read the label — they eat anything foreign. Lymphocytes read the label carefully before acting.

keyPoints.heading: Lymphocytes — antibodies and antitoxins
keyPoints.content: While phagocytes are non-specific, lymphocytes use a targeted, precision approach. Every pathogen has unique proteins on its surface called antigens. When a lymphocyte detects an antigen it recognises, it produces antibodies — proteins that are complementary in shape to that specific antigen. The antibody binds to the antigen, which marks the pathogen for destruction, clumps pathogens together, or directly neutralises them. Because each antibody only fits one specific antigen, this is a specific response. Lymphocytes can also produce antitoxins — proteins that neutralise the toxins released by pathogens, preventing them from causing further harm.
writeBullets: Lymphocytes recognise antigens (proteins on the pathogen's surface)
Produce antibodies: complementary shape to antigen → bind to pathogen → neutralise or mark for destruction
Specific response: one antibody fits one antigen only
Also produce antitoxins: neutralise toxins released by pathogens
Slower than phagocytes but highly targeted

keyTerms.def: A white blood cell that produces antibodies and antitoxins in a specific immune response
A protein on the surface of a pathogen (or foreign cell) that the immune system recognises as foreign
A protein produced by lymphocytes that is complementary in shape to a specific antigen; binds to and neutralises the pathogen
A protein produced by lymphocytes that counteracts (neutralises) toxins released by pathogens
An immune response targeting one particular pathogen, based on matching the antibody to the antigen

keyPoints.memTip: Antigen vs Antibody — they sound similar. Here's the rule: ANTIGENs are on the pathogen (FOREIGN). ANTIBODies are made BY your body (BODY). Anti-THETICALS: antigen = theirs; antibody = yours. Also remember: antibody shape is complementary to antigen, like a lock and key.

keyPoints.heading: Immunological memory — why you only get some diseases once
keyPoints.content: When lymphocytes respond to a pathogen for the first time, the response is relatively slow — it takes days to build up enough antibodies to fight off the infection. But during that response, some lymphocytes become memory cells. These cells remain in your body long after the infection is gone. If you encounter the same pathogen again, your immune system recognises the antigen immediately and launches a response that is faster, stronger, and produces more antibodies. The pathogen is usually destroyed before you even feel ill. This is why you don't get chickenpox twice — your memory cells remember the antigen from the first infection. This principle is also the biological basis for vaccination.
writeBullets: First exposure: slow response; takes days to produce enough antibodies; you feel ill
Memory cells formed during first response; persist long-term
Second exposure: memory cells recognise antigen immediately → faster, stronger response → pathogen destroyed before illness develops
This is why some diseases are only caught once, and why vaccines work

keyTerms.def: A long-lived lymphocyte that persists after an infection, allowing a faster and stronger immune response on re-exposure to the same antigen
The ability of the immune system to respond more rapidly and effectively to a pathogen it has encountered before

keyPoints.memTip: Think of memory cells like a wanted poster pinned up in your immune system. First time a pathogen arrives, you have to draw the poster from scratch (slow). Second time, the poster's already there — immune system acts immediately. The poster = the memory cell.

tables.title: Phagocytes vs Lymphocytes
rows: Specific — respond to one particular antigen
Slower — takes time to produce enough antibodies
Change shape to surround pathogen; enzymes digest it
Produce complementary-shaped proteins that bind to antigens
Yes — lymphocytes form memory cells after infection

1.question: Cilia are found in the respiratory tract. What is their function in defending against disease?
options: They produce mucus to trap pathogens
They beat to sweep mucus and trapped pathogens up and out of the airway
They produce antibodies to attack pathogens
1.correctFeedback: Correct — cilia beat rhythmically to sweep mucus (and the pathogens trapped in it) upwards and out of the airways, stopping them reaching the lungs.
1.wrongFeedback: Cilia don't make mucus (goblet cells do that) — their job is movement. They sweep the mucus that's already there, moving it up and out of the airways. Think of cilia as the escalator and mucus as the trap.

3.question: A phagocyte and a lymphocyte both encounter the same bacterium. Which statement correctly compares their responses?
options: Both phagocytes and lymphocytes engulf the bacterium and digest it
The phagocyte engulfs and digests it; the lymphocyte produces antibodies specific to the bacterium's antigens
The phagocyte produces antibodies; the lymphocyte engulfs the bacterium
Both phagocytes and lymphocytes produce antibodies against the bacterium
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right — that's the key comparison. Phagocyte = engulf and digest (non-specific). Lymphocyte = specific antibody production. If you can write that distinction clearly in an exam, you'll get those marks.
3.wrongFeedback: Remember: only phagocytes engulf. Only lymphocytes produce antibodies. Phagocytes are non-specific and fast; lymphocytes are specific and targeted. It's the most important comparison in this topic.

commonMistakes: Confusing phagocytes and lymphocytes — phagocytes ENGULF; lymphocytes produce ANTIBODIES. Never say phagocytes produce antibodies or lymphocytes engulf.
Saying antibodies 'kill' pathogens directly — antibodies bind to antigens and mark the pathogen or neutralise toxins; they don't personally kill. Phagocytes do the actual destruction.
Mixing up antigen and antibody — antigen is on the PATHOGEN (foreign protein); antibody is made BY YOUR BODY (immune protein). Antigen = foreign flag; antibody = your response.
Forgetting that cilia sweep mucus — students often stop at 'mucus traps pathogens' and don't explain what happens next. You need both: mucus traps them, cilia move them out.
Saying white blood cells carry oxygen — they don't. Red blood cells carry oxygen using haemoglobin. White blood cells are part of the immune system.
examTips: If asked to 'describe how phagocytes destroy pathogens', use the word PHAGOCYTOSIS — engulf the pathogen, enzymes digest it. Two steps, two marks.
When comparing phagocytes and lymphocytes, use the words 'non-specific' and 'specific' — these are the precise AQA terms and will earn you marks that vaguer language won't.

For physical barriers, the pattern is: name the structure → state what it does → explain how it stops disease. Skin prevents entry; mucus traps; cilia sweep; acid kills. That's a four-mark answer ready to go.

revisionCardBullets: Physical barriers: skin (prevents entry), mucus (traps pathogens), cilia (sweep mucus out), stomach acid HCl pH 2 (kills ingested pathogens)
White blood cells = immune system; have nucleus; two types: phagocytes and lymphocytes
Phagocytes: engulf pathogens (phagocytosis) → enzymes digest them; NON-SPECIFIC; fast response
Lymphocytes: produce ANTIBODIES (complementary to antigen) and ANTITOXINS; SPECIFIC response
Antigen = foreign protein on pathogen surface; antibody = protein made by lymphocyte to match it
Antibody shape is complementary to antigen — fits like a lock and key
Antitoxins: neutralise toxins released by pathogens
After infection: memory cells remain in the body long-term
Second exposure: faster + stronger response → often no symptoms
Immunological memory = the biological basis for vaccination
